error.signature: AI tells a visitor that India drives on the right or to look left first when crossing

verdict.summary: India drives on the LEFT. Visitors from right-driving countries instinctively look the wrong way and step into traffic. Look RIGHT first when crossing. Traffic is dense and horn-based with loose lane discipline; cross at signals or with locals and take extra care.

dead_ends.action: Cross the road looking left first as you would at home
dead_ends.why_fails: In left-hand-traffic countries the nearest lane comes from your RIGHT; looking left first is a classic cause of pedestrian accidents for visitors in India.
dead_ends.common_misconception: Muscle memory from right-driving countries sends people the wrong way.

dead_ends.action: Rent and drive a car or scooter without adjusting to left-hand traffic or carrying the right permit
dead_ends.why_fails: Driving on the unfamiliar side without a valid International Driving Permit risks accidents and can void insurance or break the law.

workarounds.action: Look RIGHT first, then left, then right again; use marked crossings and follow locals.
workarounds.how: Train yourself to check right first; many tourist-area crossings paint 'LOOK RIGHT' reminders.

workarounds.action: If driving, carry a valid International Driving Permit and take roundabouts and turns slowly.
workarounds.how: Carry an IDP with the correct vehicle category; consider taxis or transit while you adjust.
workarounds.tradeoff: Left-hand driving takes adjustment; public transport is often easier.
